name: Fermented Salsa
subtitle: Mexican salsa gone probiotic - tomato, chili, cilantro, alive
blrNote: Tomatoes, green chillies, onions, and coriander - all abundant year-round in BLR. This is your existing chutney ingredients, rearranged Mexican-style and fermented. At 25°C, 2 days is usually perfect.

IN.where: Any market - Indian green chillies are perfect

tldr: Dice tomatoes, chillies, onion, cilantro. Salt. Pack in a jar. 2 days later: probiotic salsa with a deep, complex tang.

steps.title: Dice and combine
steps.instruction: Dice tomatoes, finely chop chillies and onion, mince garlic, and roughly chop coriander. Combine in a bowl.
tips: Smaller dice = more surface area = faster, more even fermentation
Reserve a sprig of coriander for garnish after fermentation
Remove tomato seeds if you prefer a thicker salsa - they add extra liquid during fermentation
steps.checkpoint: Chunky, colourful salsa mix in bowl

steps.title: Salt and mix
steps.instruction: Add salt and optional lime juice. Mix thoroughly. The salt will draw moisture from the tomatoes within minutes.
tips: Taste the salsa before fermenting - it should taste well-seasoned (slightly salty is fine, it mellows)
The lime juice adds flavour but isn't necessary for fermentation
Let the salted mixture sit for 10 minutes before packing - the salt needs time to pull liquid from the tomatoes
steps.checkpoint: Juicy salsa mixture, tomato liquid pooling

steps.title: Pack the jar
steps.instruction: Transfer to a clean glass jar. Press down firmly so the salsa's own liquid rises to cover it. If needed, add a splash of brine (1 tsp salt in ½ cup water) to ensure coverage.
tips: Pack tightly - air pockets invite mould
Press a piece of cling wrap directly onto the salsa surface before loosely capping - this keeps the top layer oxygen-free and prevents kahm yeast
steps.checkpoint: Salsa submerged under its own liquid

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely. Leave at room temperature for 2–3 days. You'll see bubbles forming - this is active lacto-fermentation. Taste daily.
tips: In BLR's warmth, 2 days usually produces a perfect balance of fresh and fermented
The flavour will deepen - more complex, tangier, slightly effervescent
If it starts to smell boozy or yeasty, it's gone too long - refrigerate immediately
steps.checkpoint: Bubbling, tangy salsa with depth beyond fresh

steps.title: Refrigerate and serve
steps.instruction: Once you like the flavour, seal and refrigerate. Add fresh coriander on top. Serve with tortilla chips, on tacos, or - perfectly - with idli, dosa, or rice.
tips: The tang gets more pronounced over time - it won't stay "mild"
Stir in a splash of fresh lime juice after opening - it brightens the flavour back up after fermentation deepens it
steps.checkpoint: Complex, living salsa ready for any meal

images.caption: Fermented Salsa
images.attribution: Photo by Farhad Ibrahimzade on Unsplash
images.alt: Fermented Salsa - Mexican salsa gone probiotic - tomato, chili, cilantro, alive

culturalContext.story: Salsa has pre-Columbian roots - the Aztecs combined tomatoes, chillies, and squash seeds into sauces long before Europeans arrived. The word "salsa" is simply Spanish for "sauce." While most modern salsa is eaten fresh, lacto-fermentation adds depth, complexity, and beneficial bacteria - transforming a condiment into a living food.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Tomatoes, chillies, and coriander were all domesticated in the Americas. The global spread of salsa-like condiments happened after the Columbian Exchange. Today, salsa outsells ketchup in the United States.
culturalContext.significance: Fermented salsa bridges Mexican and Indian kitchens beautifully. The ingredient overlap - tomatoes, green chillies, coriander, onion - is almost identical to Indian chutney. The only difference is technique: Indian chutneys are ground, Mexican salsas are chunky. Fermenting either adds probiotic value.
culturalContext.funFact: Americans consume over 800 million dollars worth of salsa annually. In 1991, salsa overtook ketchup as America's top-selling condiment - a shift food historians call "the salsa moment."

thingsToAccountFor.title: Tomato quality matters
thingsToAccountFor.description: Use ripe, flavourful tomatoes - not the pale, underripe ones. Better tomatoes = better salsa. Desi tomatoes from KR Market often have more flavour than hybrid varieties.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Fast ferment in BLR heat
thingsToAccountFor.description: At 28–30°C in summer, this can ferment aggressively. Start checking at 36 hours. The line between "perfectly tangy" and "too sour" is narrow.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Texture softens
thingsToAccountFor.description: Fermentation breaks down cell walls. If you want some crunch, ferment only 1–2 days. After 3+ days, it becomes more sauce-like.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Spread fermented salsa thinly on parchment-lined trays. Dehydrate at 45°C for 10–14 hours until brittle.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Tangy salsa leather or powder - grind into a seasoning for rice, popcorn, or rub for grilled vegetables.
tips: Blend smooth before dehydrating for even drying
Makes an incredible spice powder when ground

variations.name: Roasted fermented salsa
variations.description: Char tomatoes and chillies over a gas flame before dicing. Adds smoky depth to the fermented tang.

variations.name: Mango salsa (fermented)
variations.description: Replace half the tomato with diced raw mango. Sweet-sour-spicy. Mangoes in season (Apr–Jun) make this outstanding.

variations.name: Salsa verde fermentada
variations.description: Use tomatillos instead of tomatoes if available (check Nature's Basket or grow your own). Tangier, greener.
